title: Modifying Bacteriophage    with Recombineering
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"qbkmo","text":"Recombineering is a recently developed method of in vivo genetic engineering used in Escherichia coli and other Gram-negative bacteria. Recombineering can be used to create single-base changes, small and large deletions, and small insertions in phage     as well as in bacterial chromosomes, plasmids, and bacterial artificial chromosomes (BACS). This technique uses the bacteriophage     generalized recombination system, Red, to catalyze homologous recombination between linear DNA and a replicon using short homologies of 50 base pairs. With recombineering, single-stranded oligonucleotides or double-stranded PCR products can be used to directly modify the phage     genome in vivo. It may also be possible to modify the genomes of other bacteriophages with recombineering.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":85,"length":16}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":252,"length":1},{"key":1,"offset":386,"length":1},{"key":2,"offset":668,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"vvvcs","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"\\lambda","guid":"95B51678F85A11F0A2260A58A9FEAC02"}},"1":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"\\lambda","guid":"95B51705F85A11F0A2260A58A9FEAC02"}},"2":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"formula":"\\lambda","guid":"95B51750F85A11F0A2260A58A9FEAC02"}}}}
